Reich, and Thiago Gonçalves‐Souza. 2026. “Functional Diversity Drives Leaf Decomposition Across Rainfall–Disturbance Gradients, but Phylogenetic Effects Vary.” Ecosphere 17 (6). https://doi.org/10.1002/ecs2.70653.

title: What’s in this bundle
text:
What’s in this bundle
Reconstructed from your old cv.html / contact.html / index.html (which were themselves Quarto output, so this is a faithful port):

_quarto.yml – site/navbar/footer config
index.qmd – Home page
cv.qmd – CV page
contact.qmd – Contact page
research.qmd, publications.qmd, teaching.qmd, supervisions.qmd – placeholders (your navbar links to these, but you didn’t upload the old HTML for them, so fill these in or send them to me)
styles.css – minimal starter (your old custom styles.css wasn’t uploaded, so re-add any custom CSS rules here)
images/, files/ – empty folders, see below

title: Research Interests
text:
I am a general ecologist and evolutionary biologist whose research spans broad questions at the intersection of ecology, evolution, and biodiversity science. My ongoing research is centered on the following themes:

The biology of extinction
Why do some species survive environmental change while others go extinct? To tackle this central question at the interface between ecology and evolution, I use data science tools to weave together diverse strands of evidence—fossils, global wildlife surveys, and the tree of life. This is currently being conducted at the Swain Lab, as part of my Postdoctoral Fellowship at U. of Michigan’s Institute for Global Change Biology
You can watch me presenting the preliminary results of this project here.



Caption: A preview from some of my PhD results, were I estimated for the first time the spatiotemporal components of species persistence across the biota of Australia. Colder colors show regions that promote the persistence of certain organismal groups. I am currently preparing this manuscript for publication.




Speciation and the origins of biodiversity
How and why did life become so diverse? I address this question by exploring the gap between micro- and macroevolution and investigating whether processes within populations give rise to the structure of biodiversity across the broadest scales of time and space.
My work related to this topic was recently published on The American Naturalist and in Ecology Letters.
You can watch me presenting the preliminary results of a new project I am conducting on speciation, which I recently presented as part of the symposium “Cross-Disciplinary Approaches for Understanding Budding Speciation” that I co-organized with Bruno do Rosario Petrucci in the 2026 Evolution Meeting: link for the whole symposium here.



Caption: A research program aimed at dissecting variation in speciation rates across major organismal groups, which many hpothesize may be driven by a currently unknown, rate-limiting step of the speciation process. While comparative studies often focus on all species within a clade, an equally important pattern is the phylogenetically independent variation in speciation rates across lineages, as shown in Januario et al. (2025, American Naturalist). Figure from the introduction of my dissertation.




Comparative demography
How do ecological and evolutionary processes interact to shape a population’s ability to persist, adapt, and spread across space? Do these demographic properties vary among species at the same scale as their traits, or are they more—or less—conserved? Answering these questions is a central goal of my research.
My work related to this topic was recently published in Ecology Letters and in The American Naturalist.



Caption: Results from my PhD chapter comparing the metapopulation dynamics of bottom-associated fishes across the North American Pacific and Atlantic continental shelves. Metapopulation dynamics of whole fish assemblages differ between regions. Figure 2 in Januario et al. (2025, Ecology Letters).




New methods and software in Ecology and Evolution
Exploring new research directions often requires developing new methods to extract and organize information from primary natural history data. Furthermore, modern software allow us to ask questions at scales that earlier naturalists could hardly have imagined. I build tools that help turn those possibilities into reality.
To some extend I am always building new tools, but perhaps my most prominent published, standalone software is paleobuddy, an R ecosystem to research budding speciation which I co-developed with Bruno do Rosario Petrucci. We are growing this ecosystem slowly but steadily, so reach out if you have any ideas for collaboration.



Caption: Preview of an upcoming method that combines insular occurrences with geological data to quantify and compare species persistence across fundamentally different ecological contexts, illustrated using data for the eastern quoll (Dasyurus viverrinus).




Phylogenetic natural history
What does biodiversity actually look like when we step back and try to describe it in general terms? What are its most universal patterns, and at which spatial or evolutionary scales do they emerge? And why do these patterns sometimes change when we compare small groups of organisms to much larger ones? There are many of these fundamental questions on scale that deeply interest me and inspire the way I test hypotheses about the natural world.



Caption: Molecular phylogenies of virtually all well-known organisms on Earth become more imbalanced (asymmetric) as we include broader and broader organismal groups. The causes of this extremely consistent pattern remains unknown to scientists. Figure from my dissertation introduction.




The evolution of complexity
How does the co-variation among traits change over time? And how important are those changes for shaping the diversity of life we see today?
My work on this topic is still in progress, but my recent review of the MIT Press Evolvability book provides a glimpse into how I approach these questions.



Caption: This figure shows how traits change over time within species (colored polygons) belonging to the same fossil genus. The primary data for this project was concluded and the analysis is ongoing.

title: Teaching
text: For the past 12 years, I have taught ecology and evolution courses in Brazil and the United States as a teaching assistant in multiple different courses related to Ecology and Evolutionary biology. I also presented a few workshops in different places that generated a fair amount of education material. Here I am slowly compiling some of the resources I’ve produced:

title: Teaching
section: Evolved: open software for teaching evolutionary biology
text:
Evolved: open software for teaching evolutionary biology
evolved, an open-source R package for undergraduate and graduate teaching in evolutionary biology. It provides tutorials with clear learning objectives focused on data visualization, hypothesis testing, and simulation-based reasoning using real datasets, enabling students to explore both microevolutionary processes (e.g., selection and drift) and macroevolutionary patterns from molecular and fossil data. The software stable version is available on CRAN, the development version can be found in my github. It was published in the Journal of Open Source Education and received the 2026 T. H. Huxley Award for education resources from the Society for the Study of Evolution.
Please reach out if you would like to use evolved. Some of the investigation prompts are intentionally not publicly available online, but I’m happy to share this material with any human interested in using the software to teach evolutionary biology.

title: CV
text:
International

Peer Review
Evolutionary Journal of the Linnean Society; Journal of Biogeography; Journal of Open Source Education; Methods in Ecology and Evolution; Proceedings of the Royal Society B Biological Sciences; Science Advances; Systematic Biology


Organizer of Research synthesis working group on budding speciation
Co-organized with Bruno do Rosario Petrucci (Iowa State University). This group is supported by the Society for the Study of Evolution (SSE) and is designed to serve as a launchpad for novel ideas, new syntheses, and ongoing collaborations. The group involves researchers from USA, Brazil, Canada, Germany, and Australia.


Conference mentor for early-career researchers
2026 Mentored 10+ PhD students during the SSE Postdoc Fellowship Networking event at the 2026 evolution meeting. 2026 Mentored PhD student Gustavo Martins as part of Evolution’s Bilingual Mentoring Program. 2024 STRATA (Supporting Tomorrow’s Researchers and Today’s Advocates) mentor during the North American Paleontological Convention.

title: CV
text:
Departmental

Student representative in department committees
2025–2023 Peer-to-peer mentoring of 4 younger grad students ( “Big/Little Sib program”) 2025–2026 Social Hour (“BioBev”) coordinator 2024 Admissions committee 2022–2023 Social & Retreat Committee 2015-2016 Student Representative in the Departmental Faculty Meeting (University of Sao Paulo)


Organizer of academic events for the Univ. of Michigan’s Dept. of Ecology & Evol. Biology
2-working day visit from prof. Kjetil Lysne Voje, from the Natural History Museum of the University of Oslo (Norway). Visit included advanced group discussion, a public roundtable, a seminar to the department, and individual meetings with faculty, students, and other researchers from the university. Organization included scheduling, flights, lodging and meals, all done by Matheus.
Round table: Is macroevolution more than “microevolution writ large?” (~50 attendants, 90 minutes), Featuring profs Kjetil L. Voje (U. of Oslo, Norway), L. Lacey Knowles (EEB, U. of Michigan), Lucas N. Weaver (EARTH, U. of Michigan), and Daniel L. Rabosky (EEB, U. of Michigan). Matheus organized, selected, and clarified questions from the dept. before the event, and mediated the live discussion.

title: CV
text:
United States

ID day at the Museum of Natural History of the University of Michigan (2023/2024/2025) / Science Communicator
Single day activity, Ann Arbor (MI) - USA Engaged the public by identifying species, showcasing live animals, 3D models, museum specimens, and other biologic material. Participated as member of the division of Reptiles & Amphibians at the University of Michigan’s Museum of Zoology.


BIO 173 visit to division of Reptiles & Amphibians at the U. of Michigan’s Museum of Zoology (2024) / Museum Educator
Single day activity, Ann Arbor (MI) - USA Toured tens of students from “Introduction to biology” (BIO 173) through the Reptiles & Amphibians division of the University of Michigan’s Museum of Zoology—promoting hands-on scientific observation and student engagement.


University of Michigan Biological Station’s 2024 BioBlitz / Associate organismal group expert
Single day activity, Pellston (MI) - USA Participated in an intensive biodiversity survey documenting species within the biological station, integrating fieldwork, education, and public outreach. Collaborated with students, researchers, and community members to promote biodiversity awareness and hands-on scientific engagement.

title: CV
text:
Brazil

EcoEscola 2019 (Eco-School) / Lecturer, Pedagogical leader, and class developer
March 2018 - August 2019, Sao Paulo - Brazil I coordinated an Ecology course at the University of Sao Paulo, training graduate students in active learning strategies to teach Ecology to recent biology graduates. I also designed pedagogical materials and delivered three lectures: “Patterns and Processes in Ecology,” “Interaction Biology,” and “Evolutionary Ecology and Biogeography.”


Estacao Biologia (Biology camp) / Instructor and class developer
March 2012 - November 2013, Sao Paulo - Brazil I contributed to a non-formal education project teaching Biology in underprivileged schools using active learning. I developed lasting activities, including one on micro and macroevolution. I also participated in “Bio na Rua” (Biology on the Streets), an annual community outreach event in Sao Paulo’s underserved areas.
